speaker: interviewer
text: It's February 7, 1996. The survivor is Manny Stenberg, M-A-N-N-Y S-T-E-N-B-E-R-G. The interviewer is Renee Firestone, F-I-R-E-S-T-O-N-E. We are in West Hills, California. And the language will be English. It is February 7, 1996. Testimony is given by Manny Stenberg, M-A-N-N-Y S-T-E-N-B-E-R-G, born S-Z-T-A-J-N-B-E-R-G. The interviewer is Renee Firestone, R-E-N-E-E F-I-R-E-S-T-O-N-E. We are in West Hills, California. And the language will be English.

speaker: interviewee
text: --just we lived to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mr. Stenberg-- or good afternoon. Would you please state your name and spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Manny Stenberg, M-A-N-N-Y S-T-E-I-N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Hirsch Mendel Steinberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you spell Steinberg then?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-Z-T-A-J-N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you-- when were you born? What's your birth date? And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born May, 1925, in Radom, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old does that make you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Make me 70 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your first recollection about your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were-- my father was a tailor. We weren't the most richest people in the world. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mother was very beautiful. And we were three brothers. And we lived in a two-bedroom apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you tell me about your apartment, what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father was Chaim Sztajnberg. And my mother's name was Milka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father had a shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked from the house. He worked-- you know, the shop was in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember good things about it. We weren't the richest people. But I remember very happy things, though. I remember I used to play football. I remember I was playing soccer. I remember my father used to go out and buy certain, like, salami for us, like, corn beef, rye bread, and bring it home-- or chocolate milk. We were very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father an affectionate man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very affectionate. He loved his children. And he, he was a very good person. I loved him very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any interaction with him that included only you and him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was too young for those kinds of things. I know one thing-- I know. I was very-- I was more close to my grandfather. My mother's father was very close, you know. My father was a very busy man. He was a tailor, you know. He was-- he had the light of the ladies' tailor, you know. He used to be his own designer. He used to make his own fashions, you know. He used to get fashion from Paris, you know. And he used to design everything himself, you know. People came to him from all over to make-- to make his clothes, you know. And as far as being close, I don't-- we were close to family. But interaction-- I don't think so-- I had any.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, I-- she died of cancer when I was nine years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when she died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died in 1934. I remember I came home from cheder. And I found her laying on the floor, dead. She died in bed, you know. And during the fact, I remember there was-- there were two candles which lay in, in the-- in the living room-- she, she died in the living room-- the feet at the door, covered. And there was a man sitting here. And there were two candles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you understand immediately what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, I, I knew it. I saw that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your brothers there at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was seven years ago, my brother. I'm sure he was there, too. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My younger brother was only about, about a year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the day after you came home from the funeral.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very, very sad. I, I felt the world came to an end. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt that-- as though I have nobody, nobody to turn to. I was lonely. I was crying. I was very depressed, yes? I didn't play anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I went to public school. And I went to cheder, the Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back after the funeral to school, what did your friends say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, all the-- all the neighbors from [NON-ENGLISH] were sorry for us, you know. They gave us food, you know. They, they used to-- they used to taught-- they go on to treat a sermon. Because my mother was a very likable person. Boy, she was the only sheitel maker in the city. She was making sheitels for other people. So she was very known.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was a businesswoman?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a businesswoman, yeah. My father was a tailor. And she was working on sheitel-- making sheitels.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back to school after that, did you tell your friends about it? Or did they know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the city was not-- we lived in a, a Jewish quarter. And my friends were all-- were very young. And they didn't realize what was going on. But the older people, they knew what was going on. Because she-- my mother was very known in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were-- my father-- my, my, my father was a traditional Jew. And they believed in certain traditional ways, you know, yontif, you know. They didn't work on Saturdays, no. But to go every day to temple or something else-- no, he was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still remember your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --conducting Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I used to-- my mother would light the candles, you know, and buy the big challah-- and cooking the gefilte fish, you know, and, and, and, and giving me the cholent to go up to the-- to the-- to the baker, you know, and put in the sticker on the top, you know, the sticker on the top, the, the-- On the pot we put a sticker-- we put, you know, the number, you know. Because we paid it on Friday. Until Sunday there was no pay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what the cholent was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cholent was a, a certain kind of potatoes, you know, and kasha, you know. And they put in some meat, you know, like a concoction of a lot of different foods.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how come you had to take it to the bakery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Saturday the Jewish people didn't cook. And so the baker put it in on a low fire Friday. It cooked the whole night till Saturday morning about 11 o'clock at the temple. So we went at 11 o'clock or 11:30. And we, we went there. We showed him the number. And he gave it to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the school you went to, were there any non-Jewish kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a all-Jewish school. It was all Jewish people, my, my, my public school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother went to a-- to a non-Catholic school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a lot of friends-- a few friends-- many friends. I had a few friends, you know, neighbors, you know, from the building, you know, that may be the, the janitor's kids, no? Or maybe-- somebody that may be on the first floor. They're some non-Jewish people. But I used to get-- I used to play together soccer. And so there was no difference between me and them.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did it become back a, a difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Poland, you know, because Polish are different. A yontif, you know, on yontif-- on a non-Jewish yontif, you know, like Christmas, or like New Year's, you know, or Easter became very unbearable for Jewish to go on the streets. Because they blamed the Jews for killing Jesus, you know. So we were punished for it. We couldn't go over to the non-Jewish neighborhoods. Because right away we were kicked, you know, by them or beaten or thrown stones.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember that as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, many times I was thrown stones at me and called different names, you know, definitely. Even when I was 9 and 10 years old, I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have any non-Jewish customers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a few. Quite a few. They used to come to the house. And he used to measure them, you know. Yeah. Not many, but I don't know-- I, I never signified between a non-Jew or Jew, you know, for a customer. I never checked them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you first realize that there was a difference between you and the non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I realized-- I realized when they called me a 'dirty Jew.' So I realized that I'm, I'm not-- them. I'm different.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when that first happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably about 11, 11 years old. It was getting-- it will be two years after my mother died, you know. And I began to acclimate myself to a different kind of life, you know, adjust myself to a-- I used to get [INAUDIBLE] but very lonely, though. I was trying to be one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers and I were very close, you know. All my brothers were very close, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names were?

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger brother was 22 years younger than me. His name was [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. And he was born in 1927. And my very youngest brother, his name was Abraham Jakob. He was born in 1933, a year before my mother passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived in what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Radom, R-A-D-O-M, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my, my actually-- my apartment or my house?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes-- the house and the apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived on the second floor. It was an apartment. When we walked in-- we walked in, we had a big kitchen, like a-- you know, like a mud kitchen where they-- then, to the right side, there was a bed that we-- my brother and I slept in the same bed, you know. And further, in the same room-- it was large room, remember-- and further, my father had his-- had the [POLISH], which means, you know, he was-- he had his shop in the same room. He had about two or three people working there. But it was a tremendous big room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then to the left, you know, there was a living room. And the living room also contained the bedroom. We had a collapsible-- we had a collapsible-- what do you call it? A, a couch. It opened up, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and then we had a big-- I remember that we had a big closet and mirror. So people, when they tried on their clothes, they could look in the mirror. They could look in. We had certain-- we had a big table there. Everything happened in that particular bedroom combining with the living room.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your happiest recollection as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Happiest? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, being together with my brother-- and with my brothers and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after your mother died?

speaker: interviewee
text: I began to, to dwindle. I began to think. I began to write something, like the poems, you know. I began to lay at night, you know, and think about certain different things. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I began to [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] wonder what the next day would bring and when my father would marry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want your father to marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I needed somebody in the house, you know, because my father didn't actually give us all the attention. We were very wild, you know. We didn't-- he, he couldn't take care of the house because he was very busy. And, and he-- he did-- he needed somebody to help us in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who took care of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather. My grandpa stayed with us, my mother's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your grandfather then?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 70-- must have been an old man. I remember him old. Maybe he was about seventies, maybe 60, 70, very old man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother passed away before, before my mother passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And eventually, did your father remarry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. One time I was getting up to go to school-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I don't know, I didn't pay attention. But I heard my father was get-- after he put the, the [NON-ENGLISH], which means the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what's that-- what do you call [NON-ENGLISH] in Jewish, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Gravestone?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: The gravestone?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gravestone. So I felt-- and after he put the gravestone, he had like-- I think the Jewish people believed in one year, you know, of mourning. You know, he's got a right to go ahead and go find somebody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to school and noticed there was somebody else in the house. And at first, I was very, very disturbed. I felt that somebody invaded the property.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody invaded my mother's place, even though it was not this lady's fault. She didn't kill her. But then I'm sure it's human nature. And me being a child, you know, I thought this lady was involved. Now, how stupid I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your brothers take it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-- same way, like I did, you know. He was also-- he was younger than me. He was-- he was-- he was 10 years-- he was eight years old. I was 10. It was a year before-- my mother died when I was nine. My brother was seven. And same thing-- he didn't. But thank goodness that my-- this lady took care of my youngest brother, who was a year old. She took care of him. Because she-- I was told later on she was not able to have children. So she was quite happy that she had came into a ready-made family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did they marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: They must have married in '35. Because they were only married a few years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your grandfather remain in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather remained in my house, you know, until he passed away. She allowed, my stepmother, allowed that, yeah, like a-- like a-- she was a very good person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your grandfather die?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather died-- I don't know, maybe it was about 1938, something-- right before the war, he passed away. '37-- I can't remember exactly. But I know he-- I was about 12 or 13 years when he passed away. He used to take me every night to temple, you know, and say Kaddish, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaddish?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandfather's death.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, tremendously. I was so broken up, you know, when he died, you know. I felt like, like the world came apart, like everything fell on me. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've-- I was very close to my grandfather. A lot of things, you know, he did that made me laugh-- and sometimes made me cry. Like one time, when I came back from school and there was a-- he saw me one time. So it was more in the city. So he saw me. And when I went in, I had $0.10 in my pocket, 10 [NON-ENGLISH]. And I walked into a store to buy a piece of sausage, non-Jewish sausage. But it smelled so nice, you know. And I was stupid, you know, because-- for doing that. He caught me. And all the way home, he, he me in my grabbed my ear. And he carried my home by my ear. [LAUGHS] It was funny. And he-- and all the way, he talked to me. Don't you ever do that. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your stepmother following the traditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was a traditional Jewish, you know. Her father was a very known man. He, he came-- her father, in 1905, was with Herzl. He went to Switzerland. And he was on the-- on the-- on the meeting between it with Herzl. He was-- he was a very influential person with the Zionist organization. And, in fact, we had a picture in our-- in our living room with her father and Herzl, you know, and Balfour-- all these places, who took care-- Zionists, you know-- that he was a part of it. And I remember that like-- and I was very proud of it, her being that. And she was a very nice person. She was more educated than my father. My father was not the most educated person in the world. He had finished school, knew how to write and read, you know. But she was more intelligent because she went to gymnasium. And she had a bit more education than my father.

words.text: he was a part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was able to take invoices, you know, write and-- maybe somebody didn't pay a, a bill. So she sent the bill out, you know. And I found her to be very intelligent, a very, very affectionate person, I would say. How much--

speaker: interviewee
text: We never had a problem with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much schooling did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started to go to school when I was about seven years old. I went until about-- till the war-- till the war. I went-- mostly, I went 8 o'clock in the morning until about 3 o'clock. I went to public school, learned subjects, you know, like what it's required by law. And then from there, I came home, had a sandwich. And I went by the cheder to learn Hebrew, learn, learn the Seder, learn everything about Jewishness. So I came home 7:00 at night every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: The public school was also a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The public school was not a Jewish school. It was run by the government. But mostly Jewish people were in that public school. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. After a big Jewish writer it was named. It was not a Jewish-- it was-- it was a-- it was a what do you call it? A, a regular public school by, by the state.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was it conducted in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, strictly Polish. Polish, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in your home, what language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: We spoke mostly Jewish, mostly Jewish. Occasionally we spoke Polish. Not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amongst yourselves, the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The children, amongst yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Jewish, you know. Maybe if you-- if you-- if you find a non-Jewish person, we spoke, of course, Polish. But I spoke good-- I spoke good Polish, you know. I spoke Polish. But in the house, we spoke Jewish, you know. And that's what I understood. And that's what my father-- and grandfather, especially, wanted to talk only Jewish. He was very, very Jewish-minded and Jewish traditional-minded, you know. My, my grandpa is a very old-fashioned man.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your home, was there ever conversation about anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. My father, every day when he used to get the newspaper, he used to stress the fact-- look over here what happened. Look over here. Look at this country. Look how bad it was here. Look how they're beating the Jews. Look how they-- they-- you can't go on the train to go from one city to the next city because they would beat you up, you know. They will hit you. They will-- they will close your stores, you know. And we noticed something that stores were coming in-- of non-Jewish men-- were coming in the garments industry-- opened up stores, selling clothes. This never-- we, we never seen it before. But the Jews were in charge of that, you know. And they came in. And they were, like, showering away-- or chasing away the Jewish people who were in Rowne, you know. And they took over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you father plan to do something about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father didn't-- he didn't need to do because he would just-- he didn't, didn't-- there's no way to, to do anything. We didn't have-- the Jewish people didn't actually, actually have too much power in Poland. Because we were like second citizens. And so whatever, whatever-- we were accepted, you know. We were, like-- we were-- we were, like, passive people, very passive, you know. We didn't believe in, in fighting for everything-- frantic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't think of going away from Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Many times, I wish he would have done it, you know. He would have saved our life, you know. But we weren't able because my father wasn't-- you know, we were-- we were day-to-day Jewish people. We made a living, you know. Like they say in Jewish, you know, he was working seven days, you know, and seven days, you know. He was trying to make a living in seven days. [LAUGHS] He always missed a day to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother, your stepmother, who had this connection to the Zionist leadership--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her, her, her father-- her father, not she-- not her. Her father was a Zionist movement leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: And didn't she know?

speaker: interviewee
text: She knew about it. But she wasn't involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did your life really change because of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my life changed, you know, when I-- when one time I was trying to go-- when I found out I'm being disliked. One time I went out. There, there were three or four movies were in Radom-- five movies, you know. We couldn't go to certain movies. We couldn't go because they wouldn't let Jews in. We couldn't go to certain stores. We couldn't go to a certain bank. We couldn't go certain sections where they-- of the church. We couldn't go to certain streets-- if there was a lot of non-Jewish people live, you know. It wasn't common for a Jewish person to walk around the streets like this, you know, because you probably would get beaten up by youngsters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably '38. And I was already 13 years old. My-- I had an uncle that lived probably about a couple of miles-- we lived, you know. And I used to-- and I used to go up there. I used to look all over, look around if somebody's going to do something to me. Very seldom-- there were also parks. We used to go to a park, you know. And I was afraid to go in the park because maybe I might get beaten up there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me you played soccer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, only with Jewish people. The Jewish people, we used to play soccer, you know. And we used to play chess, you know. I used to love to make little small shows. And it was very nice to put on shows, you know. We used to invite-- but we used to charge $0.10 a ticket. We used to come in. And my brother used to-- used to put on some kind of funny thing, you know, and dressed up somebody, like a clown, be like a clown. you know. We used to sell lemonade on the street, you know. We used to sell bagels on the street, you know, to make-- just to make money.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it that you first heard about war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we, we were quite afraid when he heard that the Germans Jews are being thrown out from, from Germany and being chased into Poland, of course. And when I saw something-- when I began to see new faces in my school, I noticed right away that something-- a change going on. Because they chased out the Jews from Germany. And they came to Poland. And they assigned them to my classes. And then the papers were all telling-- you know, we used to get Jewish papers, [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH], and different kind of papers, [NON-ENGLISH], and different kind of papers, you know. My father used to read them. I used to read Jewish, too. So I used to understand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make--

speaker: interviewee
text: My neighbor-- my neighbor had one. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make friends with these refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I, I had one friend. His name was Gerhard, you know. And one name was-- I remember-- and I used-- and there was a Jewish fellow. At one time, I took him home-- I was-- I took him home to my house, you know, as a blond, Jewish young fellow, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember he came home. We made my-- we shared some food with him together. And then we-- I took him one time to the-- to the-- there's a-- there was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they kept the children, the [NON-ENGLISH], which means the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] is called the-- how do you say [NON-ENGLISH] in Jewish-- in, in, in English, you know? 'Orphanages'-- I took him to an orphanage camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I showed him how-- I used to love to go orphanage places, for some reason, and observe how the children live, you know, who have no parents. After school, I used to like to go there sometimes, you know, Sunday. I didn't go Saturday. But I'd go to school. I used to love to go there and watch other children, how they are without their parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you question these refugee children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't question. I just observed them, you know. I felt kind of sorry for them. I wouldn't-- didn't have the ability to question them either. And they thought I was probably crazy, you know. But I was very-- always used to be very sorry for them how come they have no parents. And I am-- I'm only one. I'm so lucky, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop for a minute now. We're going to change tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
